affordances.name: Cross-check causal layers with multiple models
affordances.mechanism: When one disciplinary frame keeps missing incentives, trade-offs, or second-order effects, use a latticework to cross-check causal layers while translating each model into concrete tests and decision pressure.

use_when: A single disciplinary frame keeps missing important incentives, trade-offs, or second-order effects.
The decision spans several causal layers and benefits from cross-checking one model against another.
The case needs broader judgment rather than deeper expertise inside one local toolset.
do_not_use_when: People are collecting model names as intellectual decoration.
The latticework is used as a substitute for expertise.
Too many models overload working memory and obscure the actual decision.
case_evidence_needed: The local frame that is missing something important.
The second-order effect, trade-off, or incentive another model reveals.
The concrete test, comparison, or decision pressure each model adds.
A reason the selected models are useful for this case rather than decorative.

treatment_requirements.description: Require the answer to translate each nominated model into a test, comparison, or decision implication so latticework does not become name-dropping.
evidence_required: The missing incentive, trade-off, or second-order effect.
The concrete test or comparison the model suggests.
The decision pressure created by the cross-check.
good_output_shape: Names the limits of the first frame.
Adds only models that reveal case-relevant structure.
Turns each model into a test or comparison.

treatment_requirements.description: Require the answer to test only the few model cuts that change the case, prune dead-end or low-relevance models, and keep the remaining set small enough to improve judgment.
evidence_required: The candidate model cuts being considered.
The test, comparison, or hypothesis each cut changes.
The dead-end, redundant, or low-relevance models to prune.
The focused execution pressure created by the retained models.
good_output_shape: Names the small set of model cuts worth testing.
States why each retained model changes the case.
Explicitly sets aside dead-end or decorative models.
Converts the remaining models into decision pressure instead of a model pile.

diagnostic_questions: What does the current frame miss: incentives, trade-offs, second-order effects, or causal layer?
Which model adds a concrete test rather than just a new label?
Are we broadening judgment or merely collecting model names?
Which candidate model cuts should be pruned because they do not change the hypothesis, comparison, or decision?
misuse_guards: Do not use a model name unless it changes a test, comparison, or decision implication.
Do not treat surface familiarity with many models as expertise.
Do not overload the packet with a model list that weakens the actual handoff.
Do not keep dead-end model cuts after they fail to add case-specific decision pressure.

source_evidence.source_quote: single disciplinary frame keeps missing important incentives, trade-offs, or second-order effects
decision spans several causal layers and benefits from cross-checking one model against another
collect model names as intellectual decoration
testing several "cuts" at a problem using frames from different disciplines to see which one yields the most insight
pruning off dead-end analyses and focusing on confirming, disproving, or modifying hypotheses
Overloading the mind with too many models or too much complexity can impair clear thinking

review_notes.normalization_note: Batch 17 outcome: strong_affordance_record. The source supports cross-model judgment only when models become concrete tests or comparisons.
dropped_material: Did not extract a blanket instruction to use many models.
Did not treat the latticework as a substitute for domain expertise.
open_questions: Later packet review should test whether this card is useful as a meta-card or too broad for candidate packets.

absence_records.reason: The source directly warns against collecting model names as decoration; it does not support ornamental latticework.

source_evidence.source_quote: collect model names as intellectual decoration

absence_records.reason: The source uses the hammer/nail warning to reject reliance on one tool; it does not support single-model certainty in complex cases.

source_evidence.source_quote: If all you have is a hammer, everything looks like a nail
broader repertoire of tools (models) makes a person's thinking sharper

review_notes.normalization_note: Batch 17 keeps latticework as disciplined cross-checking, not a high-status model collection.
dropped_material: No runtime, prompt, lane, packet, model-ranking, or user-facing change is implied.
open_questions: A future packet should decide whether this card belongs as packet policy rather than as an ordinary candidate card.
